Q1: I tried to keep a consistent routine, usually studying around 24 hours a week spread across most days. Being an auditory learner, I'd often record myself reading notes or listen to lecture recordings while commuting or doing chores. I'd block out morning hours when my mind was clearest, but honestly, my anxiety sometimes made it hard to stick to the plan. I'd write schedules in my planner, but life kept throwing things at me, so I had to stay flexible and resilient about adjusting when needed.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and how overwhelmed I felt. I tried to maintain some regularity, like always reviewing on Sunday evenings, but deadlines would completely reshape my week. When assignments piled up, I'd abandon my normal rhythm and just focus on what was most urgent. My extracurricular commitments also meant I couldn't always stick to ideal study times, so I learned to adapt quickly.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check deadlines or see if new materials were posted. Sometimes it was triggered by getting a notification or remembering during a conversation with classmates that something was due. I'll admit, my login frequency wasn't super high—maybe 17 times over the course—because I often downloaded what I needed and worked offline. Anxiety sometimes made me avoid logging in because I didn't want to see what I was behind on.
Q4: There were brief periods where checking the LMS became more routine, especially early in the semester when I was motivated to stay on top of things. It happened when I made it part of my morning coffee ritual—just a quick check while I ate breakfast. But that habit broke down when stress peaked and I started avoiding the platform because it reminded me of everything I hadn't finished. My resilience helped me eventually return, but it never became truly automatic.
Q5: The lecture recordings and audio materials were lifesavers for me as an auditory learner. I could listen to them multiple times, pausing to take notes or rewinding parts I didn't understand. The discussion forums were also helpful because hearing different perspectives from peers helped concepts click. I watched over 250 minutes of video content because that format just works better for how my brain processes information.
Q6: I often skipped or delayed the reading-heavy PDFs and text-based resources because they didn't match my learning style. I'd download them with good intentions but then never really engage deeply with them. Some of the optional supplementary materials felt overwhelming when I was already struggling with the core content. My task completion rate suffered because I prioritized what I could actually absorb over what was technically assigned.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could clearly see how it connected to exam content or when it genuinely helped me understand something I was confused about. If I could listen to it or discuss it with peers, even better. Activities that felt disconnected from assessment or too time-consuming for unclear benefits got pushed to the bottom of my list. With medium resources and high anxiety, I had to be strategic about where I invested my energy.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford when required work was piling up. There was this constant guilt about not doing them, but my anxiety made me prioritize what directly affected my grade. Required activities carried this weight of obligation that optional ones didn't, which honestly made required tasks more stressful but also more likely to get done. I sometimes wished optional activities counted for something because they were often more interesting.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened outside the LMS. I'd download lecture audio and listen on my phone during walks or while cooking. I borrowed textbooks from the library and made handwritten notes that I'd read aloud to myself. Study groups with classmates where we'd quiz each other verbally were incredibly valuable but completely invisible to any tracking system. My 24 hours of weekly study time was mostly happening in these offline spaces.
Q10: When I was preparing for exams, I'd spend hours with printed materials and my own audio recordings, testing myself out loud. I'd pace around my room explaining concepts to myself or call my study partner to talk through difficult topics. There were whole evenings where I was deeply engaged with course content but never touched the LMS. My exam score of nearly 75% reflects learning that happened largely outside the platform.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access a lecture video once, download the audio or take detailed notes, and then work with those materials repeatedly offline. The LMS would only show that single access, but I might have revisited that content ten times in different forms. It was more convenient and didn't require internet, plus I could study in places without good wifi. My actual engagement was way higher than my login patterns would suggest.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal—my anxiety peaked and everything felt impossible. I had extracurricular commitments competing for my time, and the workload from multiple courses piled up simultaneously. There were days when just opening my laptop felt overwhelming, so I'd avoid the LMS entirely even though I knew I was falling behind. The CBT sessions I did helped me develop coping strategies, but those difficult weeks definitely showed in my assignment completion rate.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or anxious, I'd avoid the LMS because it felt like confronting all my failures at once. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars meant some weeks I barely logged in even though I was still trying to keep up through other means. Confusion about certain topics made me procrastinate on related activities because I didn't know where to start. My medium stress level was actually an improvement from earlier in the semester when it was much worse.
Q14: There were times I'd open videos or readings and just stare at them without anything sinking in. My mind would be racing with anxiety about other deadlines or life stuff, and I'd technically be 'studying' but not actually learning. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open in a tab while doing something else entirely, which probably counted as activity but wasn't real engagement. Those moments were frustrating because I was wasting time without making progress.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important—my completion rate was only about 24%, which I'm not proud of. The anxiety made starting feel impossible; I'd convince myself I needed to understand everything perfectly before attempting work. Sometimes I avoided tasks because I was afraid of failing at them, which is ironic because not doing them guaranteed a worse outcome. My resilience eventually helped me push through, but often too late to submit quality work.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that could override my avoidance patterns. I'd have bursts of LMS activity right before due dates, cramming and submitting whatever I could manage. This probably made my engagement look really inconsistent—quiet for days, then suddenly active. The deadline pressure was stressful but also weirdly helpful because it forced me past the anxiety paralysis. Without deadlines, I might have never submitted anything.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback motivated me to engage more with similar activities, but critical feedback sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me avoid that area. I appreciated detailed feedback because it helped me understand what to improve, but I'd sometimes delay reading it because I was scared of what it might say. When feedback was encouraging, it genuinely boosted my mood and made me more likely to participate in discussions.
Q18: The course structure with clear weekly modules helped me know what to focus on, which reduced some anxiety. Having lecture recordings available meant I could engage in ways that suited my auditory learning style. But when the structure felt rigid or didn't account for different learning approaches, I struggled more. The pacing sometimes felt too fast for me to properly absorb everything while managing my other commitments and mental health.
Q19: Having audio and video options made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. The discussion forums were accessible and helped me feel connected to peers. What made it harder was when important information was only in dense text documents or when navigation was confusing. Also, seeing my low task completion percentage displayed prominently increased my anxiety rather than motivating me—it just reminded me how behind I was.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying to engage but struggling to produce work that meets their own standards. Maybe they're anxious perfectionists who consume lots of content but can't bring themselves to submit imperfect assignments. Or they might be dealing with personal issues that affect their ability to complete tasks even when they're actively learning. High activity with low submission could also mean the course design doesn't translate engagement into clear output pathways.
Q21: That actually resonates with my experience somewhat. My LMS activity was relatively low, but I still earned a Merit grade because most of my learning happened offline. Students who are efficient, have good external resources, or learn well independently might not need to interact with the LMS much. It could also reflect different learning styles—someone like me who learns by listening might download once and study extensively without generating much platform activity.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when and how often someone accessed materials, and patterns like deadline-driven behavior are probably visible. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of engagement during access, and the emotional experience of learning. They can't capture conversations with peers, self-testing, or the hours spent with downloaded materials. A log might show I watched 250 minutes of video but can't show that I listened to audio versions for triple that time.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand and connect with the material, not just going through the motions. For me, it's when I'm actively thinking, questioning, and relating concepts to things I already know. It's discussing ideas with classmates and feeling curious rather than just anxious about grades. True engagement is when I lose track of time because I'm absorbed in learning, even if that happens while walking around with earbuds in rather than sitting at my computer.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show my resilience—how many times I picked myself back up after anxiety knocked me down. It can't capture the hours of audio learning while commuting, the study group sessions, or the CBT techniques I used to manage my mental health enough to keep going. It wouldn't show that my low engagement metrics masked genuine effort and learning that just happened to occur offline. My Merit grade came from invisible work that no platform tracked.
